---
title: "Lab 3 — USV Waypoint Mission Challenge"
subtitle: "Week 10 · {{< var quarter >}}"
---

## Objectives

In Lab 2 we tuned the PID "control" for the USV, including two key control loops (speed and yaw-rate) and the feedforward contribution.  In Lab 3, we will test the "overall system" performance by executing a waypoint mission with the tuned controller from Lab 2.  This is a more holistic test of the USV's closed-loop performance, since it exercises the full control stack (waypoint navigation, path planning, and low-level control) in a realistic scenario.

While the stabilization layer control (two independent single-input-single-output (SISO) compensators) is a direct example of material for the course, the added guidance and navigation layers necessary to execute the waypoint mission are more complex and less directly related to the course content.  The focus of Lab 3 is on the overall system performance, not the details of the guidance and navigation algorithms.  However, we will review the relevant sections of the ArduRover documentation to understand how the waypoint mission is executed and how it interfaces with the stabilization layer.

<!-- Internal: diagram source is site/weeks/w10_lab_usv_waypoint/images/ardurover_arch.drawio
     Edit in diagrams.net or the VS Code "Draw.io Integration" extension.
     After editing, re-export the SVG with:
       drawio --export --format svg \
         --output site/weeks/w10_lab_usv_waypoint/images/ardurover_arch.svg \
         site/weeks/w10_lab_usv_waypoint/images/ardurover_arch.drawio -->

![ArduRover architecture overview: Mission Planner (on the ground laptop) issues the mission to the autopilot, which runs the guidance, navigation, and stabilization layers on the boat. The stabilization layer is what you tuned in Lab 2; the layers above it are the focus of the [companion page](guidance_navigation.qmd).](images/ardurover_arch.svg){fig-alt="ArduRover architecture block diagram showing Mission Planner laptop above the USV's autopilot, with the mission execution / guidance / stabilization layers cascading down to the throttle and rudder actuators."}

Objectives for the week:

* **Execute a waypoint mission** with the tuned controller from Lab 2, and evaluate the overall system performance in terms of trajectory tracking and waypoint arrival.
* **Generalize the loop concepts from Lab 2** — feedforward, PID, transient and steady-state performance — to the outer guidance and navigation layers, where the same patterns reappear one level up.
* **Capture a clean mission log** of the standard challenge course (see [Challenge Course](#challenge-course) below) and report the course time both as a stopwatch reading at the field and as a log-derived estimate.

## Challenge Course

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**TBD** — instructors are finalizing the course geometry. The structure below is fixed; the specific waypoints and acceptance radius will be published before the field session.
:::

**Setup.** 3–5 waypoints on Lake Del Monte, distributed as a mission file that the planner uploads to the autopilot.

**A "run"** is one pass through the waypoints in sequence.

**Course time** is the total elapsed time from when the USV first enters the acceptance radius of waypoint 1 until it enters the acceptance radius of the final waypoint. Two measurements per run:

* Manual stopwatch (recorded by the team during the run).
* Estimated from the mission log by a provided analysis script.

Reporting both lets us see how reliably the log-based estimate matches what the team observed in real time.

### Deliverable

The main outcome of this lab is the field experience itself, so the formal deliverable is intentionally minimal. Each team submits **one PowerPoint slide** per mission log they choose to highlight, containing:

* A trajectory plot from the log, with the waypoints overlaid on the actual path.
* The course time, both stopwatch and log-derived, side by side.

At minimum, submit one slide for a clean run of the standard course. Additional slides showing variations (different `WP_SPEED`, different gain set carried over from Lab 2, etc.) are welcome but optional.

## Outline of Guidance & Navigation

See the companion page **[Guidance & Navigation — How the Waypoint Mission Works](guidance_navigation.qmd)** for the conceptual walk-through: the three-layer cascade (trajectory generation → position control → stabilization), how the S-curve generator plays the role of feedforward, and how the position controller's outputs become the speed and yaw-rate setpoints that your Lab-2 PIDs chase. That page also collects the canonical references (Leonard Hall's S-curve talks, the `AC_WPNav` / `AC_PosControl` source headers, the dev-wiki architecture docs).

Most G&N parameters are held fixed across teams for fairness — the tunable handles for Lab 3 are `WP_SPEED`, `WP_RADIUS`, `WP_OVERSHOOT`, and the kinematic caps (`ATC_ACCEL_MAX`, `ATC_DECEL_MAX`, `ATC_TURN_MAX_G`) that constrain the S-curve generator.

Key ArduPilot Rover parameter references:

* [ArduRover → Navigation](https://ardupilot.org/rover/docs/navigation.html) — waypoint navigation and path planning
* [ArduRover → Position Control](https://ardupilot.org/rover/docs/position-control.html) — how the position controller generates speed and yaw-rate targets for the stabilization layer based on current position and desired waypoint
* [ArduRover → Rover Tuning Navigation](https://ardupilot.org/rover/docs/rover-tuning-navigation.html) — tuning parameters for the navigation and position control layers

## Schedule

**Week 10 (field session):** Run the standard challenge course at least once per team, with the Lab 2 baseline gains loaded. Optional extra runs to explore the effect of `WP_SPEED` or your tuned Lab-2 gains.

**After the field session:** Generate the trajectory plot, run the analysis script for course time, and assemble the deliverable slide(s).

## Reading

Before the field session:

* **[Guidance & Navigation — How the Waypoint Mission Works](guidance_navigation.qmd)** — companion page explaining the cascade and the S-curve, framed as feedforward + PID nested one level up from Lab 2.
* **[Autopilot → Stabilization Layer](../../autopilot/stabilization.qmd)** — the Lab 2 reference. Worth re-skimming because the speed and yaw-rate setpoints the position controller hands down are exactly the channels you tuned.

## Procedure

**[Procedure](procedure.qmd)** *(to be written)* — pre-lab checklist, mission upload via Mission Planner, field workflow for a single run, in-water safety, and how to capture the stopwatch time alongside the log.

## Materials

### For Students

- Shared data repository: [ME2801_USV_Shared](https://nps01-my.sharepoint.com/:f:/g/personal/bbingham_nps_edu/IgBrVQgpC7VsQ4pnhatXVfWBAatl-so1kOaAatxqty9Bj7g?e=C0EGzJ) — same location used for Lab 2. Upload your raw `*.BIN` mission logs to your team's subfolder; instructors will convert to `.mat`.
- **Mission file** *(TBD)* — the `.mission` file with the standard course waypoints, distributed before the field session.
- **Analysis script** *(TBD)* — MATLAB script that loads a mission `.mat`, plots the trajectory against the waypoint list, and reports the log-derived course time.

### For Instructors

- Mission file authoring and upload notes — TBD.
- Course time estimation script (parses waypoint-arrival timestamps from the log) — TBD.
